Low.clinical_meaning: Low hemoglobin may indicate anemia, which can result from nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12, folate), blood loss, or chronic disease.
Low.possible_symptoms: Fatigue, weakness, pale or yellowish skin, shortness of breath, dizziness, irregular heartbeat.
Low.general_advice: Consult a doctor for further evaluation. Dietary improvements including iron-rich foods may help. Avoid self-medicating.
Low.severity_note: Values below 8 g/dL are considered severely low and require urgent evaluation.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated hemoglobin may indicate dehydration, polycythemia vera, lung disease, or living at high altitude.
High.possible_symptoms: Headaches, dizziness, blurred vision, redness of the skin.
High.general_advice: Seek medical evaluation. Ensure adequate hydration. A doctor may order additional tests.
High.severity_note: Persistently high values should be evaluated by a hematologist.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated LDL (Low-Density Lipoprotein) cholesterol increases the risk of plaque buildup in arteries, raising cardiovascular risk.
High.possible_symptoms: Often asymptomatic. In severe cases, chest pain or signs of arterial blockage.
High.general_advice: Lifestyle modifications are recommended: reduce saturated fats, increase physical activity, eat more fiber. Consult a doctor for evaluation.
High.reference_range: Optimal: <100 mg/dL | Near Optimal: 100–129 mg/dL | Borderline High: 130–159 mg/dL | High: 160–189 mg/dL | Very High: ≥190 mg/dL
High.severity_note: Values above 190 mg/dL are considered very high risk and may require medication.

Low.clinical_meaning: Very low LDL is uncommon and may sometimes be associated with hyperthyroidism, malnutrition, or certain genetic conditions.
Low.general_advice: Discuss with your doctor as it may require further evaluation in unusual contexts.
Low.severity_note: Low LDL is rarely a clinical concern on its own.

Low.clinical_meaning: Low HDL (High-Density Lipoprotein) is associated with increased risk of heart disease. HDL helps remove cholesterol from arteries.
Low.general_advice: Increase physical activity, quit smoking, reduce trans fats. Consult your doctor.
Low.reference_range: Male: >40 mg/dL | Female: >50 mg/dL | Optimal: ≥60 mg/dL
Low.severity_note: Values below 35 mg/dL represent a major risk factor for cardiovascular disease.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated HDL is generally considered protective against heart disease. However, extremely high values may warrant investigation.
High.general_advice: Maintain a healthy lifestyle. Very high HDL may be evaluated further by a doctor.
High.severity_note: Levels above 100 mg/dL may require further investigation.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated fasting blood glucose may indicate prediabetes or diabetes mellitus. The body may not be producing or using insulin effectively.
High.possible_symptoms: Increased thirst, frequent urination, fatigue, blurred vision, slow-healing wounds.
High.general_advice: Reduce sugar and refined carbohydrate intake. Increase physical activity. Consult a doctor for formal diabetes screening.
High.reference_range: Normal: 70–99 mg/dL | Prediabetes: 100–125 mg/dL | Diabetes: ≥126 mg/dL
High.severity_note: Values above 200 mg/dL with symptoms may indicate diabetes and require immediate medical attention.

Low.clinical_meaning: Low blood glucose (hypoglycemia) may result from excessive insulin, fasting, or underlying metabolic conditions.
Low.possible_symptoms: Shakiness, sweating, confusion, rapid heartbeat, anxiety.
Low.general_advice: Consume fast-acting carbohydrates if symptomatic. Consult a doctor to identify the cause.
Low.severity_note: Values below 54 mg/dL require urgent medical attention.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated creatinine may indicate reduced kidney function, dehydration, or kidney disease.
High.possible_symptoms: Fatigue, swelling in legs, decreased urination, nausea.
High.general_advice: Ensure adequate hydration. Avoid NSAIDs. Consult a doctor for kidney function evaluation.
High.severity_note: Rapidly rising creatinine may indicate acute kidney injury and requires urgent care.

Low.clinical_meaning: Low creatinine may be associated with low muscle mass, poor protein intake, or pregnancy.
Low.general_advice: Discuss with a doctor if persistent. Nutritional assessment may be helpful.
Low.severity_note: Low creatinine is less commonly concerning but warrants follow-up.

High.clinical_meaning: An elevated TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone) typically indicates an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism). The pituitary gland produces more TSH to stimulate the thyroid.
High.possible_symptoms: Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, depression.
High.general_advice: A doctor should evaluate thyroid function with additional tests (T3, T4). Treatment may include thyroid hormone replacement.
High.severity_note: TSH above 10 mIU/L often indicates overt hypothyroidism requiring treatment.

Low.clinical_meaning: Low TSH may indicate an overactive thyroid (hyperthyroidism) or excess thyroid hormone.
Low.possible_symptoms: Weight loss, rapid heartbeat, anxiety, tremors, excessive sweating.
Low.general_advice: Consult a doctor for further thyroid evaluation. Avoid excessive iodine or thyroid supplements.
Low.severity_note: TSH below 0.1 mIU/L represents significant hyperthyroidism requiring medical attention.

Low.clinical_meaning: Low platelet count (thrombocytopenia) may be linked to viral infections, autoimmune disorders, bone marrow issues, or certain medications.
Low.possible_symptoms: Easy bruising, prolonged bleeding, petechiae (small red spots on skin).
Low.general_advice: Avoid blood-thinning medications without consultation. Seek medical evaluation, especially for unexplained bruising.
Low.severity_note: Platelets below 50,000 may require medical management to prevent bleeding complications.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated platelet count (thrombocytosis) may be reactive (due to infection, inflammation, or surgery) or primary (bone marrow disorder).
High.possible_symptoms: Often asymptomatic. May increase risk of blood clots.
High.general_advice: Further evaluation recommended. Maintain adequate hydration.
High.severity_note: Counts above 1,000,000 may significantly raise clot risk.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated white blood cell count (leukocytosis) commonly indicates an active infection, inflammation, stress, or, rarely, blood disorders.
High.possible_symptoms: Fever, fatigue, body aches depending on the underlying cause.
High.general_advice: Consult a doctor for evaluation of underlying cause. Avoid self-prescribing antibiotics.
High.severity_note: Very high WBC counts may indicate leukemia and require specialist evaluation.

Low.clinical_meaning: Low WBC (leukopenia) may indicate viral infections, autoimmune disorders, bone marrow suppression, or medication side effects.
Low.general_advice: Avoid crowded places to reduce infection risk. Consult a doctor immediately.
Low.severity_note: WBC below 2,000 may indicate a serious immune deficiency requiring urgent care.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated HbA1c reflects average blood sugar levels over 2–3 months. High values indicate poor blood glucose control, consistent with prediabetes or diabetes.
High.possible_symptoms: Fatigue, frequent urination, increased thirst, slow healing.
High.general_advice: Dietary management, regular exercise, and doctor consultation are important. Medications may be adjusted if already on treatment.
High.severity_note: HbA1c above 9% indicates very poor control and high risk of complications.

Low.clinical_meaning: Low HbA1c may indicate hypoglycemia tendencies, hemolytic anemia, or blood loss.
Low.possible_symptoms: Shakiness, dizziness, confusion if hypoglycemia episodes occur.
Low.general_advice: Discuss with your doctor, especially if on diabetes medications.
Low.severity_note: Consistently low HbA1c in diabetic patients may indicate over-treatment.

High.clinical_meaning: Elevated uric acid (hyperuricemia) may lead to gout or kidney stones if persistent.
High.possible_symptoms: Joint pain (especially in the big toe), redness, swelling of joints.
High.general_advice: Reduce purine-rich foods (red meat, shellfish, organ meats), increase water intake, limit alcohol. Consult a doctor.
High.severity_note: Values above 9 mg/dL significantly increase risk of gout attacks.

Low.clinical_meaning: Low uric acid is uncommon and may be associated with certain medications or rare metabolic conditions.
Low.general_advice: Discuss with a doctor if noted consistently.
Low.severity_note: Low uric acid is rarely clinically significant.
